coredata.dc:title: Visual fluorescence detection kit for Cr (VI) based on biological matrix-derived carbon dots and assisted for AA
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Sitong
Zhang, Yuwei
Zhang, Ya
Zhang, Jinghua
Tian, Rufang
Zhao, Longshan
coredata.dc:description: In this study, a novel green donkey-hide gelatin carbon dots (D-CDs) were synthesized by hydrothermal method and a portable detector based on Cr (VI) fluorescent test strips was prepared. The synthesized D-CDs exhibited bright blue fluorescence which was used for the detection of Cr (VI) and ascorbic acid (AA). It still exhibited high sensitivity and acceptable selectivity under high concentration of other interfering ions. The morphology and elemental characteristics of D-CDs were characterized. Meanwhile, the “on-off” quenching mechanism of Cr (VI) on D-CDs was studied by ultraviolet-visible absorption spectra, fourier transform infrared spectroscopy and fluorescence spectra. Then a D-CDs/Cr (VI) fluorescence sensing platform was constructed, and reopened by AA, thus eliciting an “on-off-on” fluorescence reaction. Under the optimal conditions, the fluorescence intensity was quenched significantly with the increase of Cr (VI) concentration and restored by the good reducibility of ascorbic acid (AA), with the linear ranges of 0.5–400 μM and 1–400 μM, respectively. The detection limits of Cr (VI) and AA can be as low as 0.08 μM and 0.14 μM. Consequently, it has low toxicity and can be successfully used as a fluorescent probe to determine Cr (VI) in river water and AA in fruits with satisfactory results. It not only lays a foundation for the development of fluorescence sensor in the field of environment and food, but also provides a good application prospect for its detection of heavy metal ions and AA.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Cr (VI)
Donkey-hide gelatin carbon dots (D-CDs)
Ascorbic acid (AA)
Fluorescent probe

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst primabst ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Optical properties and stability of D-CDs
Optimization of synthesis and detection conditions for D-CDs
Study on the mechanism of fluorescence sensing
Fluorescence sensing analysis of Cr (VI) and AA by D-CDs
Selectivity and interference studies of Cr (VI)
Application of Cr (VI) probe by fluorescent test strip

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Youqing lift Program of Shenyang Pharmaceutical University
Middle-aged Backbone Personnel Training Program of Shenyang Pharmaceutical University

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the Youqing lift Program of Shenyang Pharmaceutical University ( YQ202206 ) and the Middle-aged Backbone Personnel Training Program of Shenyang Pharmaceutical University ( ZQN2016011 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM and HRTEM images. (B) Particle size distribution of D-CDs. (C) XRD spectrum and (D) FT-IR spectrum of D-CDs, (E) full scan spectra of the D-CDs (F) high resolution XPS spectrum of C 1s, (G) O 1s and (H) N 1s.

(A) The UV–vis absorption spectrum of Cr (VI) (in black line), excitation (in red) and emission (in blue) spectra of D-CDs. The slash under the curve reflected the overlap occurred to UV–vis absorption of Cr (VI) and excitation spectra of D-CDs. (B) Fluorescent lifetime of D-CDs (black line), D-CDs/Cr (VI) (red line) and D-CDs/Cr (VI)/AA (blue line). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Fluorescence emission spectra of (A) D-CDs/Cr (VI) which Cr (VI) concentration ranged from 0.5 to 400 μM and (C) D-CDs/Cr (VI)/AA which AA concentration ranged from 1 to 400 μM. Linear relationship diagrams between (B) fluorescence intensity and Cr (VI) concentration in the range of 0.5–400 μM as well as (D) AA concentration in the range of 1–400 μM.

(A) Cytotoxicity of different concentrations of D-CDs (0, 12.5, 25, 50,100, 200 μg·mL−1) on U87, HEB and HCT116 cells for 24 h. The cell image without D-CDs on U87 (B), HEB (D) and HCT116 (F). The cell image with D-CDs on U87 (C), HEB (E) and HCT116 (G).

Cr (VI) test strips: (A) under sun light and (B) under UV light (D-CDs test strip is on the far left). (C) Linear relationship diagram between R/G and Cr (VI) concentration in the range of 0–30 μM. (D) Fluorescence detection kit under sun light (on the left) and UV light (on the right) (Cr (VI)-soaked test strip is on the left and the not soaked right).

head.ce:title: Visual fluorescence detection kit for Cr (VI) based on biological matrix-derived carbon dots and assisted for AA

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Pharmacy, Shenyang Pharmaceutical University, Shenyang, Liaoning Province 110016, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Pharmacy, Shenyang Pharmaceutical University, Shenyang, Liaoning Province, 110016, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of traditional Chinese Medicine, Shandong Hongjitang Pharmaceutical Group Co., Ltd, Jinan, Shandong Province 250103, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Shandong Hongjitang Pharmaceutical Group Co., Ltd

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of traditional Chinese Medicine, Shandong Hongjitang Pharmaceutical Group Co., Ltd, Jinan, Shandong Province, 250103, PR China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Shenyang Pharmaceutical University, 103 Wenhua Road, Shenhe District, 110016 Shenyang, Liaoning Province, PR China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, a novel green donkey-hide gelatin carbon dots (D-CDs) were synthesized by hydrothermal method and a portable detector based on Cr (VI) fluorescent test strips was prepared. The synthesized D-CDs exhibited bright blue fluorescence which was used for the detection of Cr (VI) and ascorbic acid (AA). It still exhibited high sensitivity and acceptable selectivity under high concentration of other interfering ions. The morphology and elemental characteristics of D-CDs were characterized. Meanwhile, the “on-off” quenching mechanism of Cr (VI) on D-CDs was studied by ultraviolet-visible absorption spectra, fourier transform infrared spectroscopy and fluorescence spectra. Then a D-CDs/Cr (VI) fluorescence sensing platform was constructed, and reopened by AA, thus eliciting an “on-off-on” fluorescence reaction. Under the optimal conditions, the fluorescence intensity was quenched significantly with the increase of Cr (VI) concentration and restored by the good reducibility of ascorbic acid (AA), with the linear ranges of 0.5–400 μM and 1–400 μM, respectively. The detection limits of Cr (VI) and AA can be as low as 0.08 μM and 0.14 μM. Consequently, it has low toxicity and can be successfully used as a fluorescent probe to determine Cr (VI) in river water and AA in fruits with satisfactory results. It not only lays a foundation for the development of fluorescence sensor in the field of environment and food, but also provides a good application prospect for its detection of heavy metal ions and AA.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: With the constant development of industry, the emission of various heavy metal ions is increasing, which caused great harm to the environment and human health [1]. Chromium is commonly considered as the industrial raw material, mainly provided for metal plating, leather tanning, printing dye additives and catalysts [2,3]. It most exists in two states: trivalent chromium (Cr (III)) and hexavalent chromium (Cr (VI)) [4]. Cr (III) is an indispensable trace element which plays an important role to human beings [5]. Inversely, Cr (VI) is toxic and highly carcinogenic [6]. It can gradually accumulate in human body due to the massive discharge in the environment [7], so that do greatly damage to the respiratory, gastrointestinal tract and neuroimmune system [8], lead to anemia and strong stimulate to skin [9]. Therefore, it is necessary to find an effective method for the detection of Cr (VI).

In addition, ascorbic acid (AA) has also attracted a lot of attention owing to its strong antioxidant and reducing ability [10]. It is generally recognized as a key enzyme cofactor in many metabolic pathways of the human body [11]. Apart from this, it is able to prevent colds and produce vital effects in many physiological activities, such as human metabolism and improving immunity [12]. However, abnormal AA levels may bring multiple diseases, like diarrhea, scurvy [13], mental disorders [14], and kidney stones [15]. Since it cannot be synthesized in human body, the major is to obtain from the external environment such like food and medicine to protect the normal content required [16]. Naturally, it extremely expected us to detect AA in a more sensitive and accurate way.

So far, many traditional methods for the detection of Cr (VI) have been established, including atomic absorption spectrometry [17], chromatography [2], electrochemistry [18], inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry [19], and fluorescence spectrometry [20]. Similarly for the test of AA, colorimetry, capillary electrophoresis [21], chemiluminescence [22], and fluorescence method for example [23]. Nevertheless, there still accompanied by some problems, like expensive instruments and complex operation, which are difficult to be extensive applied in the actual detection [24]. At this time, fluorescence method has risen up because of its advantages of high sensitivity, simple equipment and convenient operation [4]. We are consequently supposed to develop green, low-cost and efficient fluorescent nanomaterials to stand out from them.

Nanocarbon materials refer to carbon materials with at least one dimension of dispersed phase scale less than 100 nm. Due to their advantages of high specific surface area, mechanical strength, excellent optical [25] and conductive properties, they are often used to design selective and sensitive sensors [26], and are very suitable for the detection of heavy metal ions [27,28]. Peng et al., prepared a kind of N-doped graphene quantum dots (NGQDS), and realized the rapid detection of trace Hg2+ based on the internal filtration effect [29]. Salem et al., prepared a nanocomposite material based on carbon nanotubes. In the presence of Mn7+ and Cr6+ ions, the fluorescence intensity was quenched, while Al3+ can make the fluorescence intensity recover, thus realizing the detection of heavy metal ions in aqueous solution [30].

Carbon dots (CDs) are a new kind of zero-dimensional fluorescent nanomaterials [31]. In recent years, it has been used far and wide on account of its low toxicity [32], wide source of raw materials, good water dispersion, and excellent optical properties [33]. It can be prepared by hydrothermal, solvothermal, microwave assisted method and so on [34]. Donkey-hide gelatin is a kind of traditional Chinese medicine extracted from donkey skin with a series of treatments [35]. It is rich in amino acids, collagen and trace elements, so it contains a lot of C, N and O elements. The fluorescent probe prepared by using it as the precursor material of carbon dots will not only make the material have excellent properties, but also be environmentally friendly, in line with the concept of green chemistry [36].

In this study, a new green and efficient fluorescent probe was prepared from traditional Chinese medicine donkey-hide gelatin by hydrothermal synthesis method for the detection of Cr (VI) and AA in food and water environment. The experimental results indicate that the prepared D-CDs have good biocompatibility and emit strong blue fluorescence at the excitation wavelength of 375 nm. Based on the internal filtering effect (IFE), it can be significantly quenched in the presence of Cr (VI), but in virtue of the reducibility of AA, the fluorescence can be partially recovered. A sensitive “on off on” fluorescence detection method has been established for this purpose, with the characteristics of simple operation, fast response and high sensitivity. Moreover, we studied a kind of fluorescence test strips of Cr (VI) and prepared a small detector based on it. Compared with traditional complex detector, it has low cost, simple operation, convenient portability, and is not easily affected by external light changes, achieving real-time on-site detection of Cr (VI) concentration in the actual sample.

Tecnai G2 F30 transmission electron microscope (TEM) was applied to analyze the morphology of D-CDs. The elemental composition and content of D-CDs were characterized by an Escalab250Xi X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) instrument (Thermo Fisher, USA). X-ray diffraction (XRD) spectrum was obtained from D8-Focus X-ray diffractometer (Brook, German). Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) was recorded on a Nicolet iN10 spectrometer (Thermo Fisher, USA) to study the functional group information. Ultraviolet-visible (UV–Vis) absorption spectra were performed on a UV-5500PC spectrophotometer (Shanghai Metash Instruments, China). All fluorescence spectra were obtained on F97 (Shanghai Lengguang Technology Co., Ltd. China). The fluorescence lifetime and quantum yield were measured using an PLS 980 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Edinburgh, UK).

Donkey hide gelatin powder was purchased from Pharmaceutical Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Metal cations (AlCl3, BaCl2, CdCl2, CoCl2, CuCl2, FeCl2, FeCl3, KCl, MnCl2, ZnCl2), anions (NaCl, Na2CO3, NaHCO3, Na2SO4, NaNO3, NaNO2, NaH2PO4) and sodium pyrophosphate were obtained from Hengxing Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Tianjin, China). K2Cr2O7 was purchased from Damao Chemical Reagent Factory (Tianjin, China). Ascorbic acid was provided by McLean Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Deionized water was used throughout the experiment. PBS (pH = 9) buffer was purchased from Feijing Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Fuzhou, China), and the vitamin C tablets were acquired from Northeast Pharmaceutical Group Co., Ltd. (Shenyang, China).

A green and efficient D-CDs was prepared using the traditional hydrothermal method, which were applied to detect the Cr (VI) in water environment as well as AA in fruits and vitamin C tablets. The specific synthesis steps were as follows: 0.5 g donkey hide gelatin powder was precision weighing and 10 mL deionized water was added with ultrasonic to fully dissolve it. The mixture was then placed into a baking oven at 200 °C for 9 h. After cooling to room temperature, it was transferred to a 50 mL centrifuge tube and centrifuged at 4200 r·min−1 for 15 min. The supernatant was next filtered with 0.22 μm filter membrane and freeze-dried for 48 h to obtain solid D-CDs finally.

QY is one of the important optical properties of fluorescent materials, which can be used as a measure of the luminescence efficiency. Compared with the relative QY, the absolute QY (ABS-QY) is more preferred for its wide testing range and small error, which can better reflect the fluorescence properties of the material. The ABS-QY can be expressed by the following formula:
QY = ∫
L sample
/
∫
E reference
− ∫
E sample

QY represents the absolute QY (ABS-QY), Lsample means the fluorescence emission intensity of the sample, Ereference and Esample are equal to the excitation light intensity of the reference sample and actual sample, respectively.

For the determination of Cr (VI): 0.2 g D-CDs were dissolved into 10 mL deionized water in accurate weighing to get stock solution. 200 μL D-CDs solution (20 mg·mL−1) was then mingled with 400 μL Cr (VI) solution (0.5–400 μM) in a 5 mL centrifuge tube. PBS buffer (phosphate balanced solution) (pH = 9) was taken to stabilize the volume to 3 mL. After one minute of room temperature vortex, the fluorescence spectra of the mixture were instantly measured and recorded on fluorescence detector at an excitation/emission wavelength of 375/457 nm, and the slit widths were set at 10 nm. Meanwhile, the response to other different metal cations and anions of D-CDs was carried out either to ensure its high specificity.

Roughly the same for AA, firstly, 200 μL D-CDs solution (20 mg·mL−1) was put into 400 μL Cr (VI) solution (400 μM) with a 5 mL centrifuge tube, then followed a vortex for 1 min at room temperature that resulting in the most effective D-CDs/Cr (VI) fluorescent probe. Afterwards, 1 mL AA solution (1–400 μM) was added to it with the same vortex to make thoroughly responsive, and measured at the same condition.

The river water used for Cr (VI) determination was taken from Shahe (Benxi, China), and the tap water from our laboratory. All the samples were pretreated according to the methods reported in the literature and through a properly modification [37]. The river water was firstly centrifuged at 4200 r·min−1 for 20 min to remove large suspended solids, and then the supernatant was taken and filtered by 0.22 μm filter membrane to further reduce fine particulate impurities. Tap water was boiled for 15 min to remove any interfering substances. It is noteworthy that all the samples to be tested should be stored at 4 °C and diluted 100 times with PBS buffer (pH = 9) before actual analysis.

Similarly according to the literature reports, the samples used for the detection of AA underwent a pretreatment with slightly modified [38]. The whole fresh fruits involved in the experiment were purchased from local supermarkets in Benxi, and vitamin C tablets were obtained from a local pharmacy in Benxi. The procedure was as follows: In the first place, peel and slice the lemon, orange and grape, then squeeze them into juice with a juicer. Take 10 vitamin C tablets and grind them into uniform powder with a mortar. 0.2 g powder was accurate weighing and dissolved in 20 mL pure water, ultrasound for 10 min. After that, the sample solutions were centrifuged at 4200 r·min−1 for 20 min, the supernatant was then filtered by 0.22 μm filter membrane to eliminate insoluble substances. Stored the samples at 4 °C and diluted 500 times with PBS buffer (pH = 9) for further analysis.

The cytotoxicity of D-CDs was evaluated by CCK-8 (Cell Counting Kit-8) assay. In this study, we selected three types of cells: U87, HEB, and HCT116, first inoculated the suspensions into (100 μL/well) and pre-cultured in an incubator for 24 h. Then, D-CDs solutions of different concentrations (0, 12.5, 50, 100, 200 μg·ml−1) were added to the culture plates. After incubation for a period of time, 10 μL of CCK-8 solution was added to each well and incubated in the incubator for 1–4 h. Finally, the absorbance of the cells was measured at 450 nm with an enzyme marker, and the survival rate of the cells was calculated according to the following formula:
Cell viability
%
=
As − Ab
/
Ac − Ab
× 100 %
where, As is the absorbance of the drug to be tested (including cells, medium, CCK-8 and drug solution to be tested), Ab is the absorbance of the blank solution (without cells and drug solution to be tested), and Ac is the absorbance of the control solution (without drug solution to be tested).

The morphology and structure of the D-CDs were confirmed by Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM), and the results showed a spherical D-CDs with good dispersion and high crystallinity (Fig. 1 A). As shown in Fig. 1B, the particle size was mainly distributed in the range of 10 nm, with an average particle size of 6.15 nm. Moreover, High Resolution Transmission Electron Microscope (HRTEM) images (inset in Fig. 1A) also showed that the spacing of the lattice stripes of D-CDs was 0.24 nm, which in accordance to the (002) crystal face of graphene [39]. It can be observed clearly in X-ray Diffraction (XRD) spectrum (Fig. 1C) that expressed a wide diffraction peak between 20° and 30°, along with a strong diffraction peak at 2θ = 22.9° in the center which revealing the graphite lattice spacing (002) [40], which further confirmed the results of HRTEM, showed that there existed graphite-like structures in D-CDs.

Fig. 1D was the Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FT-IR) infrared spectrum of D-CDs, which was used to identify various possible functional groups. The measurement results showed a strong and wide absorption band at 3444.07 cm−1, which may be attributed to the stretching vibration of OH and NH [41]. The infrared absorption peak at 1633.88 cm−1 was caused by the stretching vibration of CO, and the 1409.73 cm−1 by the bending vibration of CO bond and C-NH [42]. In addition, the small characteristic peak around 1139.74 cm−1 may come from the stretching vibration of C-O-C or C-OH [43]. Therefore, we speculated the presence of amide in the structure. On the other hand, there contained a lot of hydrophilic amino and hydroxyl on the surface of D-CDs, which was helpful to improve its water solubility and stability.

Beyond that, to better study the elemental composition and bond state of D-CDs, further analysis was carried out by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). As illustrated in Fig. 1E, the three characteristic peaks centered at 291.7, 404.7 and 535.8 eV could be ascribed to the binding energies of C 1 s, N 1 s and O 1 s, respectively, marking that the synthesized D-CDs contained 57.01 % carbon, 15.74 % nitrogen and 27.26 % oxygen. Fig. 1F represented the high-resolution XPS spectra of C 1 s, showing three fitting peaks at 284.7, 285.9 and 287.9 eV, which belonged to C-C/C=C, CO and CO separately, meaning the formation of unsaturated CC in the CDs. In Fig. 1G, the O 1 s spectrum could be resolved to three fitting peaks, at 530.6, 531.0 and 532.8 eV, which correspond to C-OH, CO and -OH. It also demonstrated the existence of carbonyl functional groups. Besides, the absorption peaks at 399.6 eV and 400.8 eV lead to -NH and C-NH bonds [44] (Fig. 1H). The above results were in good corroborated with FT-IR infrared spectroscopy, which agreed that large amount of amino and hydroxyl groups on the surface of D-CDs were the main reason for its good hydrophilicity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties and stability of D-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: In order to reveal the optical properties of D-CDs, we discussed its ultraviolet-visible (UV–Vis) spectrum and fluorescence spectra. As can be seen from the inset of Fig. S1A, the solution of D-CDs displayed yellow under sunlight, while turning to sharp blue fluorescence under ultraviolet light. In the UV–Vis spectrogram (Fig. S1A), a shoulder peak was found near 265 nm, which could be ascribed to the n-π* transition of CO. The fluorescence intensity was also investigated ranged from 320 nm to 510 nm (Fig. S1B) to figure out the fluorescence properties of D-CDs. It was discovered that at 375 nm excitation wavelength, the maximum emission wavelength of D-CDs was 457 nm, and with the increase of excitation wavelength, a slight red shift occurred. This excitation wavelength dependence may be impacted by the distinct size and surface states of D-CDs [45]. Concurrently, the QY of D-CDs in aqueous solution was calculated to be 4.02 %.

The fluorescence intensity was measured under different ionic strengths, pH values and long-term ultraviolet irradiation either to study the stability of D-CDs. As shown in Fig. S1C, when the NaCl concentration varied from 0 to 2 M, the fluorescence intensity basically remained unchanged, behaved a good tolerance in the high-salt environment. By evaluating the fluorescence intensity of D-CDs with the pH value from 2 to 14, it can be found that the D-CDs were basically stable in the wide range of pH 4 to 9 (Fig. S2E). In addition, the photostability was also confirmed by Ultraviolet (UV) lamp. As be seen in Fig. S1D, under the maximum excitation wavelength of 375 nm, the fluorescence intensity remained above 90 % after continuous irradiation for 90 min, indicating an excellent photobleaching resistance. We also studied the fluorescence intensity of D-CDs placed at room temperature and 4 °C for 30 days. As shown in Fig. S2F, under the room temperature for a long time, the fluorescence intensity of D-CDs will slightly decrease, while placed at 4 °C was relatively stable. Therefore, we stored D-CDs at 4 °C for more accurate measurement results. All the results showed that the D-CDs in an admirable stability and fluorescence performance, which could be popularized in practical sensing.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of synthesis and detection conditions for D-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: A fluorescent probe for the detection of Cr (VI) and AA was prepared with donkey-hide gelatin by green hydrothermal synthesis method. For the sake of the best fluorescence performance of D-CDs, the influence of different synthesis temperature and time on its fluorescence intensity during the synthesis process were explored. As illustrated in Fig. S2A, the fluorescence intensity gradually rose with the temperature within the range of 160–200 °C, and reached the maximum at 200 °C. Fig. S2B showed the effect of diverse synthesis time. It was observed that with the prolongation of time, the fluorescence intensity increased by degrees but had no change after 9 h. Therefore, the react at 200 °C for 9 h was chosen for the purpose of saving time.

For purpose of ensuring the sensitivity and best performance of fluorescent probe in practical detection of Cr (VI) and AA, the effect of reaction time, temperature and pH value likewise was studied. Fig. S2C optimized the impact of reaction time. It could be seen that after adding Cr (VI) for about 1 min, the fluorescence intensity of D-CDs decreased significantly, and then stayed basically unchanged in later 20 min, indicating that D-CDs could react with Cr (VI) rapidly, so that notable quenching could be noticed within 1 min. Similarly, when AA was added to the system, the fluorescence intensity could be substantially recovered after 1 min of reaction, and there was little change in the following 20 min, so 1 min was selected as the best reaction time. In Fig. S2D, the fluorescence intensity of D-CDs/Cr (VI) and D-CDs/Cr (VI)/AA systems had no obvious translation in the range of 20–80 °C, proving the insensitive to temperature, accordingly made it possible to react at room temperature. Even more, the association with pH value was researched on detection. As represented in Fig. S2E, the quenching degree of Cr (VI) on D-CDs reached the lowest until the pH value was 9, while AA was added to D-CDs/Cr (VI) system, which could be recovered to a certain extent when the pH value from 4 to 10. Therefore, PBS buffer (pH = 9) was taken for subsequent experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Study on the mechanism of fluorescence sensing
ce:section.ce:para: Furthermore, we experimentally investigated the quenching mechanism of Cr (VI) on D-CDs. Firstly, the UV absorption spectra of Cr (VI) and the fluorescence spectra of D-CDs were brought into comparison. As shown in Fig. 2 A, Cr (VI) gave a strong absorption peak at wavelengths between 300 and 400 nm, while the excitation and emission peaks at 375 and 425 nm were attributed to D-CDs. It was apparent that the absorption spectrum of Cr (VI) overlapped with the fluorescence spectra of D-CDs, which could be inferred there was an internal filtering effect (IFE) between them. On this count, part of the D-CDs emission light was shielded by Cr (VI), and induced the occurrence of quenching. The fluorescence lifetimes of D-CDs and D-CDs/Cr (VI) systems were also compared to deeply explore the quenching mechanism. As can be seen from Fig. 2B, the fluorescence lifetime of D-CDs only changed marginally (6.43–6.90 ns) before and after Cr (VI) was added. This phenomenon described that charge transfer did not occur to them, and corroborated the law of static quenching [38]. After Cr (VI) was added to D-CDs, the peak position of UV absorption spectrum had a certain red shift due to the coordination effect between them, which further validated the occurrence of static quenching [15]. While AA was put into D-CDs/Cr (VI) system, it could happen a distinct enhancement to UV absorption, which may be due to the damage caused by AA on the coordination effect. It is conjectured that because of the strong reducibility of AA, the fluorescence could be partially recovered by transforming Cr (VI) into Cr (III) [46]. In conclusion, the fluorescence quenching between D-CDs and Cr (VI) was arisen out of the IFE and static quenching mechanism of coordination interaction exactly. Thus, it could be well considered as an efficient fluorescence sensor.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence sensing analysis of Cr (VI) and AA by D-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 3 A delivered that under the optimal experimental conditions, the fluorescence intensity of D-CDs decreased observably with the increase of Cr (VI) concentration, and performed a good linear relationship in the range of 0.5–400 μM. The linear regression equation was as follows: y = −4.758[Cr (VI)] + 4634, R2 = 0.9932 (Fig. 3B). According to the formula 3 σ/S (σ is the deviation of 11 samples, S represents the slope of standard curve), it could be calculated that the detection limit was as low as 0.08 μM, which was permissible for the maximum concentration of Cr (VI) in drinking water of 0.96 μM stipulated by WHO.

In likewise, for the D-CDs/Cr (VI) system, the effect of different AA concentrations on it under the optimal experimental conditions was analyzed (Fig. 3C). From Fig. 3D, it could find that the fluorescence intensity in the range of 1–400 μM was linearly correlated with AA concentration (y = 3.829[AA] + 2683, R2 = 0.9984), and the detection limit was figured up as 0.14 μM.

The experimental method with previously reported detection methods of Cr (VI) and AA was paralleled (Table S1 and Table S2), and it is easily to draw a conclusion that the proposed method was not only simple and fast, but had a low detection limit, so it was supposed to be more suitable for sensing detection in real environments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity and interference studies of Cr (VI)
ce:section.ce:para: So as to evaluate the selectivity of the fluorescent probe, the effect of different metal cations (Zn2+, Ba2+, Al3+, Cd2+, Co2+, Cu2+, Mg2+, Mn2+, K+, Na+, Fe2+, Fe3+) and anions (HPO4 2−, H2PO4 −, CO3 2−, HCO3 −, SO4 2−, NO2 −, NO3 −) on the fluorescence intensity of D-CDs were summarized. As spread in Fig. S3A, all of the above cations and anions with the concentration of 1 M was added to the D-CDs solution, and it is discovered that at the maximum excitation wavelength of 375 nm, only Cr (VI) (400 μM) had significant fluorescence quenching. In the meantime, Fe3+ also behaved a certain quenching effect on D-CDs, but not as sensitive as Cr (VI). The results declared that the fluorescence probe had a better selectivity for Cr (VI). Any other, it should pay attention to avoid the possible influence of Fe3+ in the actual measurement process. The masking agents like pyrophosphate or NaF were added to it, and it is demonstrated a favorable masking effect [45].

Apart from this, the complexity of the water environment led to the possibility of multiple other ions, which required us to adopt measures for getting rid of the interference created by them. All the metal cations and anions involved in the above interference experiment was put into the D-CDs/Cr (VI) system, and it is found that no evident change happened to the fluorescence intensity. It is verified these interference substances did not affect the fluorescence intensity of the system (Fig. S3B). In brief, the fluorescent probe was in a certain selectivity and suitable for sensitive detection of Cr (VI) in practical environment.

The level of Cr (VI) in two different water environments (river water and tap water) was studied by standard addition method aimed at assessing the feasibility of the fluorescent probe in practical application. By adding different concentrations of Cr (VI) solutions (20, 100, 200 μM) to the pretreated water samples, it can be derived from table S3 that the recovery of Cr (VI) in river water and tap water ranged from 98.2 % to 105.5 %, with a relative standard deviation (RSD) lower than 3.4 %, which expressed an excellent fluorescence property.

Table S4 showed the outcome of D-CDs/Cr (VI) fluorescent probe used to detect AA in several kinds of fresh fruits and vitamin C tablets. It was measured that the content of AA in fresh oranges, lemons and grapes were 48.2, 38.6 and 25.2 μM individually, and 10.1 μM in vitamin C tablets. After AA standard solutions with different concentrations (20, 100, 200 μM) were added to them, the recoveries ranged from 89.6 % to 119.5 % and with RSD less than 3.3. These results exhibited the reliability and feasibility of the fluorescent probe, which had a broad application prospect in detecting Cr (VI) in water environment and AA in fresh fruits.

Cytotoxicity is an important criterion to evaluate biocompatibility. The survival rates of U87, HEB and HCT116 cells were measured using the CCK-8 method in the presence of D-CDs. As can be seen from Fig. 4 A, in the presence of high concentrations of D-CDs, the survival rates are still higher that all above 80 %, showing low cytotoxicity and good biocompatibility. In addition, it can also be found in Fig. 4, that compared with the control group, the overall shape of the cells was in good shape without significant changes after adding D-CDs solutions of different concentrations, which further proving that the prepared D-CDs have a certain future in the research of fluorescence sensors.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of Cr (VI) probe by fluorescent test strip
ce:section.ce:para: In order to further integrate fluorescent probe with practical applications, we investigated fluorescent test strips of Cr (VI) probe and prepared a small portable detector, which combined with a smartphone to detect the concentration of Cr (VI) in actual samples. First, we immerse the test strip in D-CDs solution for one minute and dry completely in air to form D-CDs test strips. Then, different concentrations of Cr (VI) solution were configured, and the test strips were soaked in the solution for one minute to fully react and naturally dry. Under the irradiation of ultraviolet lamp, it can be seen that the test strips emit blue fluorescence. Compared with the simple D-CDs test strip, the blue fluorescence is significantly quenched, and the quenching degree is enhanced with the increase of Cr (VI) concentration (Fig. 5 B). Under sun light, the test strips have the same appearance without obvious color change (Fig. 5A), thus confirming the feasibility of the test strips for Cr (VI) detection.

Next, we used smart phones to realize on-the-spot detection of Cr (VI) at real time. Under the irradiation of ultraviolet lamp, we collected color images through smart phone, and then used the Image J software installed on the phone to analyze the color intensity. The fluorescent images were divided into red (R), green (G) and blue (B) channels, and then the specific value of each color channel can be obtained, so as to realize the quantitative detection of Cr (VI). We found that the ratio of R/G showed a good linear relationship with the concentration of Cr (VI) within the range 0–30 μM, and the linear regression equation was y = 0.0213[Cr (VI)] + 0.7011, R2 = 0.9971 (Fig. 5C). Based on the above results, a low-cost and sensitive fluorescent test strip combined with portable smart phone can be provided to detect Cr (VI) in real time.

Considering the external factors such as strong light irradiation and others during on-site testing, which will have a certain impact on the collection of fluorescence images and cause deviation of the measurement results, we designed a small portable fluorescence detection kit, which is equipped with ultraviolet lamp belt and connected with a switch. A transparent window was provided for the surface of the test kit so that the color change of the test strip can be clearly observed though the test kit is closed (on the left of Fig. 5D). During on-site testing, we fixed the test strip soaked with Cr (VI) in the groove of the kit, and turned on the ultraviolet lamp switch. It can be observed that even under the sunlight, we can clearly distinguish the color change (on the right of Fig. 5D).

In summary, a novel “on-off-on” fluorescent probe with good water solubility and stability was first prepared by simple and economical hydrothermal synthesis method using green donkey-hide gelatin powder as raw material. Up to now, it had been used to detect Cr (VI) in water environment as well as AA in fresh fruits and vitamin C tablets with success. Under the existence of Cr (VI), the fluorescence of D-CDs was significantly quenched based on the internal filtration effect, and it could be rapidly recovered due to the reduction of AA either. This method possessed a wide linear range, and the detection limit could be as low as 0.08 and 0.14 μM. Excessively, it played a fast response speed, distinguished selectivity and sensitivity, thus it had been proved to be a superior fluorescent probe for the detection of Cr (VI) and AA. It also laid a foundation for the development of fluorescence sensors in the environmental and food fields in the future, which had an extremely extensive application prospect.

Sitong Liu: Investigation, Methodology, Software, Validation, Visualization, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Ya Zhang: Methodology, Resources, Supervision. Jinghua Zhang: Data curation, Resources, Supervision. Rufang Tian: Methodology, Resources, Supervision. Longshan Zhao: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Resources, Supervision, Validation, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: There are no conflicts to declare.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Youqing lift Program of Shenyang Pharmaceutical University (YQ202206) and the Middle-aged Backbone Personnel Training Program of Shenyang Pharmaceutical University (ZQN2016011).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.diamond.2024.111382.
